title: Discipleship, Part 1: What Is Discipleship?
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 1 - What is Discipleship_ - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. Introduction: The Church's Mission and This Series
subpoints: The church's mission is to make disciples of every nation -- the conclusion of Kevin DeYoung and Greg Gilbert's What Is the Mission of the Church?
The series will answer: What is a disciple? How are disciples made? Who is involved? What does it look like?
The church does not exist primarily to feed the poor, fight racial inequality, or dig wells -- these flow from discipleship but are not the mission itself

outline.title: 2. The Word 'Disciple': History and Meaning
subpoints: Used 269 times in the Gospels and Acts; absent from the Epistles and Revelation; John's favorite title for followers of Jesus
Originally: a person who directs his or her mind to something -- a learner (root of the word 'mathematics')
Evolved to include: dependence on an authority of superior knowledge, imitation of the teacher, and fellowship with other disciples of the same master
In the New Testament: primarily describes those who attach themselves to Jesus as their master

outline.title: 3. What Is a Disciple of Jesus? (Luke 14)
subpoints: A disciple loves Jesus more than any other person -- including family (verse 26): willing obedience even when family calls it hatred
A disciple dies to self daily -- bearing the cross means dying, not merely suffering (verse 27)
A disciple forsakes all for the sake of Christ -- at any moment willing to give up whatever is required to obey (verse 33)

outline.title: 4. How Are Disciples Made? The Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20)
subpoints: Grounded on Jesus's authority -- he was declared King of kings by the resurrection
'Make disciples' is the one command; 'go,' 'baptizing,' and 'teaching' describe how that happens
Baptizing stands for evangelism -- introducing people to Christ and the visible church
Teaching to *observe* -- not just to know, but to live out all that Christ commanded; discipleship is not merely a lecture

outline.title: 5. Definition of Discipleship and Where the Series Is Going
subpoints: Discipleship: the constant process in which a Christian is helped by the covenant community to grow in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ, so that he becomes progressively conformed to the image of Jesus Christ and can in turn disciple others
Evangelism is a subcategory of discipleship, not a separate category
Discipleship is the job of the local church, not parachurch organizations
The series will cover: marriage, parenting, singleness, education, and church relationships as discipleship contexts

summary:
This first installment of the Discipleship series introduces the series, defines the key term, and establishes the biblical foundation for disciple-making. Pastor Tito Lyro begins with a class discussion, gathering preliminary answers to questions like 'What is a disciple?' and 'What is discipleship?' before working through the Scriptures to sharpen those answers. The series is designed to complement the church's study of Kevin DeYoung and Greg Gilbert's What Is the Mission of the Church?, whose conclusion -- that the church's primary mission is to make disciples of all nations -- the series takes as its starting point.

A significant portion of the lesson traces the history of the Greek word for 'disciple.' Originally a term for a learner attached to a teacher (and the root of the word 'mathematics' via Pythagorean usage), the word accumulated meaning over time to include dependence on an authoritative teacher, learning by imitation, and belonging to a community of fellow disciples of the same master. In the New Testament, Jesus's favorite word for his followers carries all of this freight.

Luke 14:25-33 provides three marks of a genuine disciple: a love for Jesus that makes every other love look like hatred by comparison, a daily willingness to carry the cross (die to self), and a readiness to forsake all for Christ's sake. These are not advanced-level Christian goals -- they define what it means to be a Christian at all.

The Great Commission in Matthew 28:18-20 then frames how disciples are made: by baptizing (introducing people to Christ through evangelism) and by teaching to observe all that Jesus commanded. Evangelism, Pastor Lyro argues, is not a separate category from discipleship -- it is the opening chapter of it. The series closes with a comprehensive definition of discipleship and a preview of the topics ahead: marriage, parenting, singleness, education, and relational life as arenas of mutual disciple-making.
transcript:
## Discipleship, Part 1: What Is Discipleship?

*Pastor Tito Lyro -- Sunday School*

---

The rest of us, I'd love for you to turn to Luke chapter 14, starting at verse 25.

> *Now great multitudes went with him. And he turned and said to them, "If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple. And whoever does not bear his cross and come after me cannot be my disciple. For which of you, intending to build a tower, does not sit down first and count the cost, whether he has enough to finish it -- lest, after he has laid the foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him, saying, 'This man began to build and was not able to finish.' Or what king, going to make war against another king, does not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand? Or else, while the other is still a great way off, he sends a delegation and asks conditions of peace. So likewise, whoever of you does not forsake all that he has cannot be my disciple."*

We're starting a new series today on discipleship, and I think it's going to be a good one -- a longer series, because it includes quite a few things we can put under this umbrella.

Our church is also studying Kevin DeYoung and Greg Gilbert's book *What Is the Mission of the Church? Making Sense of Social Justice, Shalom, and the Great Commission*. The conclusion of the book is that the reason the church was sent into the world -- the word "mission" comes from the Latin for "sent" -- is to make disciples of Jesus Christ. Not to feed the poor, not to fight racial inequality, not to dig wells for needy communities -- though those are good causes that Christians should be involved in as disciples. But that's not the primary reason the church was sent into the world. The primary reason is to make disciples of Jesus Christ.

### Questions We'll Be Answering

Some of the questions we're going to be asking:

- What is a disciple?
- What is discipleship?
- How is a disciple made?
- Who is a disciple?
- Who should be involved in discipleship?
- Are there differences between evangelism and discipleship?

As you can see, this is a big subject involving all kinds of people and all kinds of things.

### The Word 'Disciple'

The word *disciple* is the favorite word for a follower of Jesus Christ in the Gospels and the book of Acts. It's used 269 times in those books. Interestingly, it's not found anywhere in the New Testament outside of the Gospels and Acts. In the epistles and the book of Revelation, you don't find the word "disciple" at all. But it's Jesus's favorite word to describe his followers -- and of all the New Testament authors, the one who uses it most is John in the Gospel of John alone.

The word is an old word in the original language. Early on, it was used to refer to a person who directs his or her mind to something -- a learner. It was a favorite word of the Pythagoreans (yes, the Pythagorean theorem people). They said you can explain everything in life through numbers, and so they were called disciples -- learners. The word *mathematics* is based on the Greek word for disciple.

As Greek evolved, the word came to imply a direct dependence of the one learning upon an authority of superior knowledge. Then the idea of imitation came into the meaning. In 1 Corinthians 11:1, Paul says: *"Imitate me, just as I also imitate Christ."* That's involved in the word *disciple* -- a learner who learns cognitively but also by imitation.

Finally, those described by this word existed in fellowship with other disciples of the same master. That was intrinsic to the word.

So a disciple of the Lord Jesus:
- Is a learner who learns from a master -- both by what the master teaches and what the master does
- Is in a close bond with and to the one teaching him
- Exists in fellowship with other disciples of the same master

### What Is a Disciple of Jesus? (Luke 14)

**A disciple of Jesus loves him more than any other person in his or her life.** Verse 26: *"If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple."*

Jesus doesn't mean absolute hatred. He means that if our love for him is so much greater than our love for anyone else that the comparison makes other loves look like hatred -- that is what he requires. For example: if your unsaved parents say, "You love church more than you love us!" when you choose to attend worship on the Lord's Day, your love for Christ in their eyes looks like hatred. That is what Jesus means. A disciple is willing to obey Christ even when family calls it hatred.

**A disciple of Jesus dies to self.** Verse 27: *"Whoever does not bear his cross and come after me cannot be my disciple."* Luke 9 says "take up his cross *daily*." Bearing the cross means dying, not merely suffering. Anytime we are number one in our lives, we are failing to live as a disciple of Jesus Christ.

**A disciple of Jesus forsakes all for Christ.** Verse 33: *"Whoever of you does not forsake all that he has cannot be my disciple."* At any moment where we are not willing to give up whatever it is in order to be obedient to Christ, we are not behaving like a disciple. These marks define what a Christian is, not an advanced level to aspire toward.

### How Are Disciples Made? Matthew 28:18-20

> *And Jesus came and spoke to them, saying, "All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth. Go therefore and make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all things that I have commanded you; and lo, I am with you always, even to the end of the age."*

**The command is grounded on Jesus's authority.** All authority has been given to him -- declared in its fullness by the resurrection. He is the King of kings. The church can confidently follow him because he has all authority to ask this.

**The one command is "make disciples."** The word *go* is a participle, not a command -- more accurately, "as you go" or "when you go from here." The church doesn't need to wonder what to do next: make disciples.

**Baptizing** stands for evangelism. Baptism signifies our union to Christ, our introduction to the body of Christ. The making of disciples begins by introducing the not-yet-disciples to Jesus Christ through the gospel. Evangelism is not a separate category from discipleship -- it is the beginning of discipleship. Discipleship is the bigger category.

**Teaching to observe** -- not just to know, but to live out. Jesus doesn't want people who learn lots of stuff about him and then do nothing with it. The making of disciples is not merely a lecture; it is teaching people to live out, believe, and practice all that Christ has commanded.

This disciple-making continues until Jesus returns. As long as there are people groups in the world, the church knows what it is supposed to be doing.

### Definition of Discipleship

A good definition: **Discipleship is the constant process in which a Christian is helped by the covenant community to grow in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ, so that he becomes progressively conformed to the image of Jesus Christ, and can in turn disciple others.**

Note:
- It is constant -- not occasional
- It involves *community* -- the local church, not primarily parachurch organizations
- The goal is conformity to the image of Christ, not to the image of the discipler
- The end is that disciples make more disciples

In this series we'll be talking about marriage, parenting, singleness, education, and how all of our relationships in the church are arenas of mutual disciple-making. Let's pray.
